title: Huntington's Disease
oneLiner: Autosomal dominant CAG repeat disorder causing progressive chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disturbance; onset typically 30–50 yr.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 45-year-old right-handed man is referred by his GP for "jerky movements" that have worsened over the past year. His wife reports he has become increasingly irritable, forgetful, and has trouble concentrating at work. On examination, you notice brief, random, dance-like movements of his fingers, toes, and face that he tries to incorporate into voluntary movements. When you ask him to hold his arms outstretched, you see a "milkmaid's grip." Gait reveals a wide-based, irregular stepping pattern. He has difficulty with rapid alternating hand movements, and subtle loss of saccadic eye velocity. Family history is significant: his mother died in a psychiatric hospital.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age 45** – adult-onset classically 30–50; younger = more rigid/akinetic; older = milder chorea.
- **"Jerky movements"** – chorea; brief, involuntary, flowing from one body part to another.
- **Irritability + forgetfulness** – early psychiatric and cognitive manifestations (often precede motor signs).
- **Milkmaid's grip** – inability to maintain grip due to chorea of finger flexors.
- **Wide-based, irregular gait** – combination of chorea and basal ganglia dysfunction (frontal-subcortical).
- **Loss of saccadic velocity** – early sign; slowed horizontal saccades (holds disease identifier).
- **Family history: mother died in psychiatric hospital** – autosomal dominant inheritance; psychiatric presentation misdiagnosed in prior generation.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Juvenile-onset (Westphal variant):** <20 yr. Presents with rigidity, bradykinesia, seizures, and intellectual decline – *no chorea*. Often misdiagnosed as Parkinson's or encephalitis.
- **Late-onset (>60 yr):** Chorea may be very mild; first manifestation is often cognitive. Family history may be absent because the affected parent died young (e.g., in psychiatric hospital). Misdiagnosed as vascular dementia or normal-pressure hydrocephalus.
- **Psychiatric-only prodrome:** Depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive traits, or apathy years before any motor sign. In a young adult with family history, consider Huntington's before labeling primary psychiatric disorder.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Huntington's disease (HD) is a heritable brain disorder in which an abnormal protein (mutant huntingtin) progressively destroys cells in the basal ganglia (especially the striatum) and cerebral cortex. This leads to uncontrollable movements (chorea), cognitive decline (dementia), and emotional/psychiatric changes. It is an autosomal dominant condition – if a parent is affected, each child has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation.

**Why it matters:**
- **Prevalence:** ~5–10 per 100,000; more common than often thought; every neurologist and many general physicians will see cases.
- **Clinical stakes:** Progressive, incurable, devastating; median survival 15–20 years from onset. High suicide risk (5–10%).
- **Common pitfall:** Misdiagnosed as Sydenham chorea, essential tremor, or drug-induced movement disorders. Genetic testing without counseling can be catastrophic. **Never ignore** chorea + family history.
- **Special nuance:** Predictive testing (in at-risk individuals) is a major ethical issue – do not perform without expert genetic counseling.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story:**
Huntington's disease is caused by an expanded CAG trinucleotide repeat in the *HTT* gene on chromosome 4. Repeats ≥40 cause full penetrance; 36–39 have reduced penetrance. The longer the repeat, the earlier the onset. The repeat encodes a polyglutamine tract in the huntingtin protein. The mutant protein aggregates, resists degradation, and damages neurons (gain-of-function toxicity). Key areas: caudate, putamen (striatum), and later cortex.

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
- **Chorea:** Loss of GABAergic medium spiny neurons in the *indirect pathway* of the basal ganglia (projecting from striatum to globus pallidus externus). This disinhibits the subthalamic nucleus and globus pallidus internus, leading to excessive thalamocortical drive and involuntary movements.
- **Cognitive decline:** Frontostriatal circuits are disrupted – executive function, working memory, set-shifting deteriorate. Later, cortical atrophy causes broader dementia.
- **Psychiatric symptoms:** Degeneration in limbic circuits (amygdala, orbitofrontal cortex, striatum). Mutant huntingtin also impairs neurotrophic factors (BDNF) and mitochondrial function, increasing vulnerability to depression.
- **Rigidity/akinesia in juvenile cases:** Greater involvement of the *direct pathway* (striatum to GPi), leading to parkinsonism rather than chorea.
- **Slow saccades:** Oculomotor basal ganglia circuits (caudate to frontal eye fields) are affected early.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The chorea that is "dance-like" and can be incorporated into voluntary movements (pseudowilled) reflects the chaotic disinhibition of motor cortex. The milkmaid's grip is chorea acting against a voluntary contraction. Loss of saccadic velocity is one of the earliest exam findings.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Cause Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Genetic – single cause** | Autosomal dominant mutation in *HTT* (CAG repeat expansion) | "Family history of chorea, dementia, or psychiatric illness in parent/sibling" |
| **De novo mutation** | Expansion of intermediate allele (27–35 repeats) into full penetrance (most often from paternal transmission) | "No family history; father had no symptoms but was older (>50) at conception" |
| **Reduced penetrance alleles (36–39)** | May cause mild HD or remain asymptomatic; can expand in offspring | "Mild chorea in elderly; no known family history" |
| **Not infectious, not autoimmune, not toxin (except phenotypically similar insults)** | – | (Differentiate from Wilson's, tardive dyskinesia, etc.) |

**Most common cause:** Inherited CAG expansion from an affected parent. In populations of European descent, almost all HD is due to this single cause.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis requires:
- Progressive motor disorder (chorea or rigidity) **plus** family history **plus** confirmatory genetic testing. No formal scoring system like other movement disorders, but the Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (UHDRS) is used for research/severity.

**Genetic testing:**
- **WHAT it shows:** Number of CAG repeats in *HTT*.
- **WHY:** Confirms the diagnosis definitively.
- **HOW to interpret:**
  - ≥40 repeats = full penetrance
  - 36–39 = reduced penetrance (may develop or remain asymptomatic)
  - 27–35 = intermediate (not at risk themselves, but can expand in offspring)
  - ≤26 = normal
- **Pattern recognition:** A positive genetic test **is diagnostic** regardless of clinical picture. But do not test without counseling and consent (even in symptomatic patients – inform them).

**Brain MRI:**
- **WHAT it shows:** Atrophy of caudate nucleus (bilateral, symmetric) with ex-vacuo dilatation of the frontal horns of lateral ventricles.
- **WHY:** Helps rule out mimics (e.g., vascular lesions, Wilson's) and supports diagnosis when genetic test is equivocal.
- **INTERPRET:** The "boxcar" appearance of the lateral ventricles due to caudate volume loss. Later, diffuse cortical atrophy.

**Other tests (to rule out mimics):**
- **Ceruloplasmin, serum copper, 24h urine copper:** Wilson's disease – low ceruloplasmin, elevated urinary copper.
- **Peripheral smear:** Acanthocytes in chorea-acanthocytosis.
- **Thyroid function, anti-streptolysin O titer:** Rule out thyrotoxicosis or Sydenham chorea.
- **Serum prolactin:** If chorea from neuroleptic exposure (tardive).

**Single finding that confirms:** Genetic test showing ≥40 CAG repeats. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal tri nucleotide repeat count ≤26 (assuming no testing error).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – Symptom control:**
- **Chorea:** If distressing or causing falls → tetrabenazine 12.5 mg orally twice daily (titrate up to 25 mg TID). *Why:* Depletes vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT2), reducing dopamine; first-line evidence from RCTs.
- **Agitation/psychosis:** Olanzapine 2.5–5 mg orally/IM as needed. *Why:* Atypical antipsychotic provides sedation and may reduce chorea, though weaker than tetrabenazine.
- **Depression/suicidal ideation:** SSRI (e.g., sertraline 50 mg daily) with senior review. *Why:* Suicide risk is highest early after diagnosis; treat aggressively.
- **Do not:** Give typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) long-term because they worsen tardive dyskinesia risk and can cause rigidity; use only short-term if needed.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – Multidisciplinary assessment:**
- **Physiotherapy/Occupational therapy:** Gait and balance training, fall prevention, adaptive equipment.
- **Speech and language therapy:** Assess swallowing – videofluoroscopy if silent aspiration suspected.
- **Dietitian:** High-calorie diet because chorea increases energy expenditure; screen for weight loss.
- **Genetic counseling:** For patient and family; discuss predictive testing for at-risk offspring.
- **Psychiatry referral:** Formal evaluation for depression, OCD, or psychosis.
- **Social worker:** Discuss advance care planning, care needs, driving restrictions.

**Long-term:**
- **Symptomatic treatment:**
  - Chorea → tetrabenazine (max 100 mg/day) or deutetrabenazine (newer, FDA-approved).
  - Depression → SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram, fluoxetine).
  - Psychosis → second-generation antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole).
  - Rigidity → levodopa/carbidopa (if akinetic-rigid variant, though may worsen chorea); use cautiously.
- **Supportive care:** Residential placement, home care, palliative care as disease progresses.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not prescribe levodopa for chorea (it increases dopamine, worsens movements).
  - Do not use typical neuroleptics for chronic chorea (risk of tardive dyskinesia).
  - Do not ignore weight loss or dysphagia – leading cause of death is aspiration pneumonia.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Huntington's Disease | Wilson's Disease | Sydenham Chorea | Drug-induced Tardive Dyskinesia |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| **Age** | 30–50 (adult) or <20 (juvenile) | 5–30 | 5–15 | Any (after chronic neuroleptic use) |
| **Movement quality** | Chorea (brief, flowing), may incorporate voluntary actions | Chorea, dystonia, tremors | Chorea (rapid, irregular), plus hypotonia | Orofacial stereotypies, chorea, dystonia |
| **Cognitive/psychiatric** | Dementia, depression, irritability | Behavioral changes, depression, cognitive decline; personality change typical | Emotional lability, OCD-like behavior | Usually no cognitive decline; may have anxiety |
| **Eye findings** | Slow saccades, later ophthalmogenesis | **Kayser-Fleischer rings** (corneal copper deposition) | None | None |
| **Systemic** | Weight loss, falls, dysphagia | Liver disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), hemolysis, renal | Recent streptococcal pharyngitis, rheumatic fever | History of chronic antipsychotic/ antiemetic use |
| **Family history** | **Autosomal dominant** (50% risk) | Autosomal recessive | Not familial | Not familial |
| **MRI findings** | **Caudate atrophy**, ex-vacuo ventriculomegaly | Basal ganglia T2 hyperintensities, "face of giant panda" sign | Usually normal | Normal or nonspecific |
| **Labs** | Genetic test (CAG repeats) | Low ceruloplasmin, elevated urinary copper, liver enzymes | Elevated anti-streptolysin O, ESR | Normal (except history) |
| **Key differ** | Genetic test + caudate atrophy | Kayser-Fleischer rings + low ceruloplasmin | Pediatric + post-streptococcal | Chronic neuroleptic exposure + orofacial predominance |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complications:**
- **Falls and injuries (including head trauma):** Chorea causes instability. *Key signs:* Repeated fractures, subdural hematoma. *What to do:* Assess gait, provide walking aid, consider tetrabenazine.
- **Aspiration pneumonia:** Dysphagia develops as disease progresses (especially with chorea of pharyngeal muscles). *Key signs:* Coughing while eating, recurrent chest infections, weight loss. *What to do:* Swallow assessment, thickened fluids, feeding tube (PEG) if high risk.
- **Suicide:** Highest in first few years after diagnosis; especially in those with preserved insight. *Key signs:* Withdrawn, giving away possessions, expressed hopelessness. *What to do:* Urgent psychiatric review, crisis plan, remove means.
- **Severe rigidity/akinesia (juvenile HD):** Can lead to contractures, pressure sores, rhabdomyolysis. *What to do:* Passive range of motion, positioning, treat with levodopa (cautiously).
- **Seizures:** More common in juvenile HD (up to 30%). *What to do:* Anti-epileptic drug (e.g., levetiracetam – avoid phenytoin due to possible exacerbation of chorea).

**Late presentation patterns – "what happens if untreated":**
- **Decompensated HD:** Patient arrives bedbound, mute, cachectic, with severe choreoathetoid movements, flexion contractures, and pressure ulcers. This is end-stage. Manage palliatively – pain, secretions, dyspnea.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**
- **CAGE** for HD symptoms: **C**horea, **A**taxia/poor coordination, **G**ait disturbance, **E**motional/psychiatric.
- **Huntington's Triad: Motor, Cognitive, Psychiatric (MCP)** – all three are necessary for full diagnosis; many present with just one or two early on.
- **Treat Chorea with TETRA** – **T**etrabenazine, **E**xcellent for chorea, **T**itrate slowly, **R**eview for depression, **A**void typical neuroleptics.

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Chorea improves with alcohol** – a classic but not pathognomonic finding (also seen in Sydenham chorea). Ask about it.
2. **"Milkmaid's grip"** – ask patient to squeeze your fingers; chorea causes grip to release intermittently.
3. **Huntington's patients often have slowed horizontal saccades** before any other sign – test it.
4. **Weight loss without apparent cause** in a middle-aged adult should prompt chorea search (especially if family history of "dementia").
5. **Depression in HD responds poorly to tricyclics** – use SSRIs like sertraline or citalopram.
6. **Juvenile HD is almost always from paternal transmission** due to genetic anticipation (larger expansions in sperm).
7. **Never inform a patient of their genetic test result without a genetic counselor in the room** – risk of suicide is high.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Mistaking chorea for restlessness or anxiety** – watch for incorporation into voluntary movements.
- **Prescribing antipsychotics for chorea without considering tetrabenazine** – typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) can cause tardive dyskinesia (worse chorea) and parkinsonism.
- **Forgetting to ask about family history** in a structured way (e.g., "Did anyone in your family have unusual movements or a psychiatric illness that didn't get better?").
- **Ordering genetic testing without consent/counseling** – this can have devastating consequences for the patient's family.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any sign of sepsis (especially aspiration pneumonia) – ↑ lactate, ↓ urine output.
- **GCS drop <13** – consider intracranial bleed (fall) or status epilepticus (especially juvenile).
- **Severe agitation with aggression** – risk to self or others; consider sedatives, senior psychiatric review, possible section.
- **Suicidal intent or plan** – urgent crisis psychiatry; patient may need 1:1 nursing.
- **Sudden onset of inability to swallow or stridor** – acute airway compromise from severe dysphagia; call anaesthetist.
- **Status epilepticus** (juvenile HD) – follow seizure protocol; call senior neurologist.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** in setting of infection/aspiration – early sepsis recognition.
- **Desaturation <90%** on room air or new oxygen requirement – get chest X-ray, consider NIV (if appropriate).
- **New focal neurological deficit** – head CT to rule out subdural haematoma (common fall injury in HD).
- **Decompensated swallowing** – if water swallow test fails, request immediate speech and language therapy and consider NG tube.

**Remember:** End-of-life decisions should be discussed early. When disease progresses to immobility and mute state, focus on palliative care, not aggressive ICU measures unless reversible. Involve palliative care team for symptom control (pain, secretions, agitation).

## Huntington's Disease

**One-liner:** Autosomal dominant CAG repeat disorder causing progressive chorea, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disturbance; onset typically 30–50 yr.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 45-year-old right-handed man is referred by his GP for "jerky movements" that have worsened over the past year. His wife reports he has become increasingly irritable, forgetful, and has trouble concentrating at work. On examination, you notice brief, random, dance-like movements of his fingers, toes, and face that he tries to incorporate into voluntary movements. When you ask him to hold his arms outstretched, you see a "milkmaid's grip." Gait reveals a wide-based, irregular stepping pattern. He has difficulty with rapid alternating hand movements, and subtle loss of saccadic eye velocity. Family history is significant: his mother died in a psychiatric hospital.

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Age 45** – adult-onset classically 30–50; younger = more rigid/akinetic; older = milder chorea.
- **"Jerky movements"** – chorea; brief, involuntary, flowing from one body part to another.
- **Irritability + forgetfulness** – early psychiatric and cognitive manifestations (often precede motor signs).
- **Milkmaid's grip** – inability to maintain grip due to chorea of finger flexors.
- **Wide-based, irregular gait** – combination of chorea and basal ganglia dysfunction (frontal-subcortical).
- **Loss of saccadic velocity** – early sign; slowed horizontal saccades (holds disease identifier).
- **Family history: mother died in psychiatric hospital** – autosomal dominant inheritance; psychiatric presentation misdiagnosed in prior generation.

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
- **Juvenile-onset (Westphal variant):** <20 yr. Presents with rigidity, bradykinesia, seizures, and intellectual decline – *no chorea*. Often misdiagnosed as Parkinson's or encephalitis.
- **Late-onset (>60 yr):** Chorea may be very mild; first manifestation is often cognitive. Family history may be absent because the affected parent died young (e.g., in psychiatric hospital). Misdiagnosed as vascular dementia or normal-pressure hydrocephalus.
- **Psychiatric-only prodrome:** Depression, anxiety, obsessive-compulsive traits, or apathy years before any motor sign. In a young adult with family history, consider Huntington's before labeling primary psychiatric disorder.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Huntington's disease (HD) is a heritable brain disorder in which an abnormal protein (mutant huntingtin) progressively destroys cells in the basal ganglia (especially the striatum) and cerebral cortex. This leads to uncontrollable movements (chorea), cognitive decline (dementia), and emotional/psychiatric changes. It is an autosomal dominant condition – if a parent is affected, each child has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation.

**Why it matters:**
- **Prevalence:** ~5–10 per 100,000; more common than often thought; every neurologist and many general physicians will see cases.
- **Clinical stakes:** Progressive, incurable, devastating; median survival 15–20 years from onset. High suicide risk (5–10%).
- **Common pitfall:** Misdiagnosed as Sydenham chorea, essential tremor, or drug-induced movement disorders. Genetic testing without counseling can be catastrophic. **Never ignore** chorea + family history.
- **Special nuance:** Predictive testing (in at-risk individuals) is a major ethical issue – do not perform without expert genetic counseling.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story:**
Huntington's disease is caused by an expanded CAG trinucleotide repeat in the *HTT* gene on chromosome 4. Repeats ≥40 cause full penetrance; 36–39 have reduced penetrance. The longer the repeat, the earlier the onset. The repeat encodes a polyglutamine tract in the huntingtin protein. The mutant protein aggregates, resists degradation, and damages neurons (gain-of-function toxicity). Key areas: caudate, putamen (striatum), and later cortex.

**Mechanism -> Sign:**
- **Chorea:** Loss of GABAergic medium spiny neurons in the *indirect pathway* of the basal ganglia (projecting from striatum to globus pallidus externus). This disinhibits the subthalamic nucleus and globus pallidus internus, leading to excessive thalamocortical drive and involuntary movements.
- **Cognitive decline:** Frontostriatal circuits are disrupted – executive function, working memory, set-shifting deteriorate. Later, cortical atrophy causes broader dementia.
- **Psychiatric symptoms:** Degeneration in limbic circuits (amygdala, orbitofrontal cortex, striatum). Mutant huntingtin also impairs neurotrophic factors (BDNF) and mitochondrial function, increasing vulnerability to depression.
- **Rigidity/akinesia in juvenile cases:** Greater involvement of the *direct pathway* (striatum to GPi), leading to parkinsonism rather than chorea.
- **Slow saccades:** Oculomotor basal ganglia circuits (caudate to frontal eye fields) are affected early.

**Connecting to pattern recognition:** The chorea that is "dance-like" and can be incorporated into voluntary movements (pseudowilled) reflects the chaotic disinhibition of motor cortex. The milkmaid's grip is chorea acting against a voluntary contraction. Loss of saccadic velocity is one of the earliest exam findings.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Cause Category | Specific Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Genetic – single cause** | Autosomal dominant mutation in *HTT* (CAG repeat expansion) | "Family history of chorea, dementia, or psychiatric illness in parent/sibling" |
| **De novo mutation** | Expansion of intermediate allele (27–35 repeats) into full penetrance (most often from paternal transmission) | "No family history; father had no symptoms but was older (>50) at conception" |
| **Reduced penetrance alleles (36–39)** | May cause mild HD or remain asymptomatic; can expand in offspring | "Mild chorea in elderly; no known family history" |
| **Not infectious, not autoimmune, not toxin (except phenotypically similar insults)** | – | (Differentiate from Wilson's, tardive dyskinesia, etc.) |

**Most common cause:** Inherited CAG expansion from an affected parent. In populations of European descent, almost all HD is due to this single cause.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis requires:
- Progressive motor disorder (chorea or rigidity) **plus** family history **plus** confirmatory genetic testing. No formal scoring system like other movement disorders, but the Unified Huntington's Disease Rating Scale (UHDRS) is used for research/severity.

**Genetic testing:**
- **WHAT it shows:** Number of CAG repeats in *HTT*.
- **WHY:** Confirms the diagnosis definitively.
- **HOW to interpret:**
  - ≥40 repeats = full penetrance
  - 36–39 = reduced penetrance (may develop or remain asymptomatic)
  - 27–35 = intermediate (not at risk themselves, but can expand in offspring)
  - ≤26 = normal
- **Pattern recognition:** A positive genetic test **is diagnostic** regardless of clinical picture. But do not test without counseling and consent (even in symptomatic patients – inform them).

**Brain MRI:**
- **WHAT it shows:** Atrophy of caudate nucleus (bilateral, symmetric) with ex-vacuo dilatation of the frontal horns of lateral ventricles.
- **WHY:** Helps rule out mimics (e.g., vascular lesions, Wilson's) and supports diagnosis when genetic test is equivocal.
- **INTERPRET:** The "boxcar" appearance of the lateral ventricles due to caudate volume loss. Later, diffuse cortical atrophy.

**Other tests (to rule out mimics):**
- **Ceruloplasmin, serum copper, 24h urine copper:** Wilson's disease – low ceruloplasmin, elevated urinary copper.
- **Peripheral smear:** Acanthocytes in chorea-acanthocytosis.
- **Thyroid function, anti-streptolysin O titer:** Rule out thyrotoxicosis or Sydenham chorea.
- **Serum prolactin:** If chorea from neuroleptic exposure (tardive).

**Single finding that confirms:** Genetic test showing ≥40 CAG repeats. **Single finding that rules out:** Normal tri nucleotide repeat count ≤26 (assuming no testing error).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – Symptom control:**
- **Chorea:** If distressing or causing falls → tetrabenazine 12.5 mg orally twice daily (titrate up to 25 mg TID). *Why:* Depletes vesicular monoamine transporter (VMAT2), reducing dopamine; first-line evidence from RCTs.
- **Agitation/psychosis:** Olanzapine 2.5–5 mg orally/IM as needed. *Why:* Atypical antipsychotic provides sedation and may reduce chorea, though weaker than tetrabenazine.
- **Depression/suicidal ideation:** SSRI (e.g., sertraline 50 mg daily) with senior review. *Why:* Suicide risk is highest early after diagnosis; treat aggressively.
- **Do not:** Give typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) long-term because they worsen tardive dyskinesia risk and can cause rigidity; use only short-term if needed.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – Multidisciplinary assessment:**
- **Physiotherapy/Occupational therapy:** Gait and balance training, fall prevention, adaptive equipment.
- **Speech and language therapy:** Assess swallowing – videofluoroscopy if silent aspiration suspected.
- **Dietitian:** High-calorie diet because chorea increases energy expenditure; screen for weight loss.
- **Genetic counseling:** For patient and family; discuss predictive testing for at-risk offspring.
- **Psychiatry referral:** Formal evaluation for depression, OCD, or psychosis.
- **Social worker:** Discuss advance care planning, care needs, driving restrictions.

**Long-term:**
- **Symptomatic treatment:**
  - Chorea → tetrabenazine (max 100 mg/day) or deutetrabenazine (newer, FDA-approved).
  - Depression → SSRIs (sertraline, citalopram, fluoxetine).
  - Psychosis → second-generation antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole).
  - Rigidity → levodopa/carbidopa (if akinetic-rigid variant, though may worsen chorea); use cautiously.
- **Supportive care:** Residential placement, home care, palliative care as disease progresses.
- **What NOT to do:**
  - Do not prescribe levodopa for chorea (it increases dopamine, worsens movements).
  - Do not use typical neuroleptics for chronic chorea (risk of tardive dyskinesia).
  - Do not ignore weight loss or dysphagia – leading cause of death is aspiration pneumonia.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Huntington's Disease | Wilson's Disease | Sydenham Chorea | Drug-induced Tardive Dyskinesia |
|---------|----------------------|------------------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| **Age** | 30–50 (adult) or <20 (juvenile) | 5–30 | 5–15 | Any (after chronic neuroleptic use) |
| **Movement quality** | Chorea (brief, flowing), may incorporate voluntary actions | Chorea, dystonia, tremors | Chorea (rapid, irregular), plus hypotonia | Orofacial stereotypies, chorea, dystonia |
| **Cognitive/psychiatric** | Dementia, depression, irritability | Behavioral changes, depression, cognitive decline; personality change typical | Emotional lability, OCD-like behavior | Usually no cognitive decline; may have anxiety |
| **Eye findings** | Slow saccades, later ophthalmogenesis | **Kayser-Fleischer rings** (corneal copper deposition) | None | None |
| **Systemic** | Weight loss, falls, dysphagia | Liver disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis), hemolysis, renal | Recent streptococcal pharyngitis, rheumatic fever | History of chronic antipsychotic/ antiemetic use |
| **Family history** | **Autosomal dominant** (50% risk) | Autosomal recessive | Not familial | Not familial |
| **MRI findings** | **Caudate atrophy**, ex-vacuo ventriculomegaly | Basal ganglia T2 hyperintensities, "face of giant panda" sign | Usually normal | Normal or nonspecific |
| **Labs** | Genetic test (CAG repeats) | Low ceruloplasmin, elevated urinary copper, liver enzymes | Elevated anti-streptolysin O, ESR | Normal (except history) |
| **Key differ** | Genetic test + caudate atrophy | Kayser-Fleischer rings + low ceruloplasmin | Pediatric + post-streptococcal | Chronic neuroleptic exposure + orofacial predominance |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complications:**
- **Falls and injuries (including head trauma):** Chorea causes instability. *Key signs:* Repeated fractures, subdural hematoma. *What to do:* Assess gait, provide walking aid, consider tetrabenazine.
- **Aspiration pneumonia:** Dysphagia develops as disease progresses (especially with chorea of pharyngeal muscles). *Key signs:* Coughing while eating, recurrent chest infections, weight loss. *What to do:* Swallow assessment, thickened fluids, feeding tube (PEG) if high risk.
- **Suicide:** Highest in first few years after diagnosis; especially in those with preserved insight. *Key signs:* Withdrawn, giving away possessions, expressed hopelessness. *What to do:* Urgent psychiatric review, crisis plan, remove means.
- **Severe rigidity/akinesia (juvenile HD):** Can lead to contractures, pressure sores, rhabdomyolysis. *What to do:* Passive range of motion, positioning, treat with levodopa (cautiously).
- **Seizures:** More common in juvenile HD (up to 30%). *What to do:* Anti-epileptic drug (e.g., levetiracetam – avoid phenytoin due to possible exacerbation of chorea).

**Late presentation patterns – "what happens if untreated":**
- **Decompensated HD:** Patient arrives bedbound, mute, cachectic, with severe choreoathetoid movements, flexion contractures, and pressure ulcers. This is end-stage. Manage palliatively – pain, secretions, dyspnea.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**
- **CAGE** for HD symptoms: **C**horea, **A**taxia/poor coordination, **G**ait disturbance, **E**motional/psychiatric.
- **Huntington's Triad: Motor, Cognitive, Psychiatric (MCP)** – all three are necessary for full diagnosis; many present with just one or two early on.
- **Treat Chorea with TETRA** – **T**etrabenazine, **E**xcellent for chorea, **T**itrate slowly, **R**eview for depression, **A**void typical neuroleptics.

**Clinical pearls:**
1. **Chorea improves with alcohol** – a classic but not pathognomonic finding (also seen in Sydenham chorea). Ask about it.
2. **"Milkmaid's grip"** – ask patient to squeeze your fingers; chorea causes grip to release intermittently.
3. **Huntington's patients often have slowed horizontal saccades** before any other sign – test it.
4. **Weight loss without apparent cause** in a middle-aged adult should prompt chorea search (especially if family history of "dementia").
5. **Depression in HD responds poorly to tricyclics** – use SSRIs like sertraline or citalopram.
6. **Juvenile HD is almost always from paternal transmission** due to genetic anticipation (larger expansions in sperm).
7. **Never inform a patient of their genetic test result without a genetic counselor in the room** – risk of suicide is high.

**Common exam/ward mistakes to avoid:**
- **Mistaking chorea for restlessness or anxiety** – watch for incorporation into voluntary movements.
- **Prescribing antipsychotics for chorea without considering tetrabenazine** – typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) can cause tardive dyskinesia (worse chorea) and parkinsonism.
- **Forgetting to ask about family history** in a structured way (e.g., "Did anyone in your family have unusual movements or a psychiatric illness that didn't get better?").
- **Ordering genetic testing without consent/counseling** – this can have devastating consequences for the patient's family.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior / ICU / crash team:**
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or any sign of sepsis (especially aspiration pneumonia) – ↑ lactate, ↓ urine output.
- **GCS drop <13** – consider intracranial bleed (fall) or status epilepticus (especially juvenile).
- **Severe agitation with aggression** – risk to self or others; consider sedatives, senior psychiatric review, possible section.
- **Suicidal intent or plan** – urgent crisis psychiatry; patient may need 1:1 nursing.
- **Sudden onset of inability to swallow or stridor** – acute airway compromise from severe dysphagia; call anaesthetist.
- **Status epilepticus** (juvenile HD) – follow seizure protocol; call senior neurologist.

**Specific thresholds:**
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** in setting of infection/aspiration – early sepsis recognition.
- **Desaturation <90%** on room air or new oxygen requirement – get chest X-ray, consider NIV (if appropriate).
- **New focal neurological deficit** – head CT to rule out subdural haematoma (common fall injury in HD).
- **Decompensated swallowing** – if water swallow test fails, request immediate speech and language therapy and consider NG tube.

**Remember:** End-of-life decisions should be discussed early. When disease progresses to immobility and mute state, focus on palliative care, not aggressive ICU measures unless reversible. Involve palliative care team for symptom control (pain, secretions, agitation).
